segments.s599-g49: چنین گفت کز گنج من یک زمان is a reporting clause: چنین گفت (“he said thus”) introduces a following content clause begun by کز (archaic contraction of که + ز). کز here functions like “that/from which” introducing the clause about the speaker’s treasure; یک زمان (literally “one time”) means “for a while/for the moment.” Word order is typical Persian: report + کز + topic (گنج من) + temporal phrase (یک زمان).
segments.s599-g50: نیم شاد کز مردم شادمان: نیم + adjective gives a partial/limited quality (“half/partly happy” or “not wholly happy”) and shad is the predicate adjective. The following کز again introduces the reason/clause ‘that/from the people…’; مردم شادمان is a verbless descriptive phrase “the people being joyful.” The sequence is elliptical: ‘I am not fully happy, that the people are glad.’
segments.s599-g51: نیم خواستار سرای سپنج: نیم modifies خواستار to indicate partial desire (“somewhat/partly desirous”). خواستار is an adjectival/participial form meaning “desirous (of).” سرای + سپنج describes the object wished for (“a house/place of sponge/soft repose”); note that سپنج here evokes softness/rest (poetic). The head-noun (خواستار) precedes its object phrase in this compact poetic order.
segments.s599-g52: نه از بازگشتن به تیمار و رنج: نه marks the negative contrast “not”; از introduces the infinitival clause بازگشتن (“to return”) and its object is به تیمار و رنج (“to care/concern and hardship”). بازگشتن is an infinitive used as a noun (verbal noun) after از, so the whole phrase means “not because of / not from returning to care and suffering.”
segments.s599-g53: که آنست جاوید و این ره‌گذار: که is the conjunction/relative “that,” linking to the previous statement; آنست is the copula construction (آن + ست) “that is,” and جاوید is the predicate adjective “eternal.” The coordinate و joins the clause about این ره‌گذار (“this one is a passer-by/transient”), giving the contrast eternal vs. transient in balanced poetic phrasing.
segments.s599-g54: تو از آز پرهیز و انده مدار: تو is the (emphatic) vocative/subject “you.” از + noun (آز) marks what to avoid, and پرهیز is a verbal noun used as an exhortation (“refrain/abstain”). The second conjunct انده مدار is an imperative admonition “do not be sorrowful” (اندَه/اندوه = sorrow; مدار here functions to form the negative exhortation); the clause thus gives two commands addressed to “you.”
segments.s599-g55: به پنجم چنین گفت کز رنج کس: به پنجم (“to the fifth”) indicates the addressee/recipient of the speech. چنین گفت (“thus he said”) is the reporting formula; کز introduces the content clause “that…” and رنج کس is a genitive/construct phrase “the suffering of someone/another.” The order places the addressee first, then the report and the concern about another’s suffering.
segments.s599-g56: نیم شاد تا باشدم دست‌رس: نیم شاد again expresses an incomplete/limited happiness (“half-happy” / “not wholly glad”). تا is the temporal conjunction “until,” and باشدم is an archaic subjunctive/formal present (“that I may be / that I should be” i.e. “until I am”). دست‌رس is the predicate meaning “within reach/able to reach or to help.” Together: “I will not be (even partially) glad until I am able to reach (to help).”
segments.s599-g81: پذیرفتم is past tense, 1st person singular: 'I accepted/acknowledged.' آن را is a demonstrative + object marker ('that' as direct object). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/which / who'). فرمان is the noun 'command/order.' برد here forms the idiom فرمان برد(ن) 'to obey an order' (classical use of بردن as a light verb); this specific sense of برد is an idiomatic meaning ('obey/take') rather than any literal 'victory' or 'gain'. The subordinate clause modifies آن and completes what was accepted.
segments.s599-g82: گناه begins the question: 'what sin/blame...?' آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying سگالد. سگالد is a participial/agentive form referring to one who employs dogs or harasses (literally 'one who sets dogs on / harrier'); that word here denotes the person who did the attacking. که introduces the relative clause 'who/that'. درمان برد is 'took a remedy/received treatment' (درمان = cure/remedy; برد again is used in the sense 'to take/receive' or as part of a light-verb construction). The whole segment is a rhetorical question about blame.
segments.s599-g83: نشستم is past tense 1st person singular 'I sat' (classical Persian: نشستن). بر + این (orthographically shown as برین) gives the locative 'upon this.' تخت is 'throne.' فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' modifying پدر; together فرخ پدر = 'the fortunate father' or 'my auspicious/illustrious father,' so the phrase denotes 'this throne of the fortunate father.' Word order places the verb before its locative phrase, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s599-g84: بر here functions idiomatically as 'in/according to' when combined with آیین: بر آیین = 'in the manner/way of' or 'following the custom of.' آیین is 'custom/ceremony/practice.' طهمورث دادگر is a name plus epithet: 'Tahmuras the just,' and this phrase specifies the manner (i.e. 'in the fashion of Tahmuras the just'). This prepositional phrase modifies the action 'I sat' (indicating model or tradition).
segments.s599-g85: به داد is an adverbial phrase 'in regard to justice / in justice.' از نیاکان 'than/from my ancestors' establishes the comparison or source ('beyond my ancestors' or 'over what my ancestors did'). فزونی is a noun 'increase' used here in a comparative/augmentative sense. کنَم is the 1st person present/subjunctive form used to express intention or future action ('I will make/increase'). Overall: 'I will increase justice beyond my ancestors.'
segments.s599-g86: شما را is the direct object with the enclitic object marker را ('you' as the ones addressed). به دین is a prepositional phrase 'in/to the religion/faith.' رهنمونی is a deverbal noun meaning 'guidance/instruction' and together with کنَم ('I will make/do') yields an action: 'I will guide/instruct.' Word order puts the object and prepositional phrase before the verb, with کنم final as the finite verb expressing the speaker's intention.
segments.s599-g87: جز ... نیست is a common classical Persian formula meaning 'there is nothing except ...' (i.e. 'only ...'). Here 'جز از راستی نیست' literally 'there is nothing except from truth' = 'there is only truth'. The postpositional phrase 'با هرکسی' ('with anyone') marks the scope (with whom this rule holds). Word order places the negated existential verb 'نیست' after its exception phrase (typical in Persian).
segments.s599-g88: 'اگر چند' functions concessively = 'even if / if though'. 'ازو' is the fused preposition+pronoun (از + او) meaning 'from him/it'. 'کژی آید' is a simple-present/clause 'a wrongness/fault comes' (آید = 'comes' here). The final adverb 'بسی' (= 'enough / sufficient') comments on the whole conditional clause: 'if some fault comes from him, that is sufficient.'
segments.s599-g105: نهادند is the past tense, 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/put' (they placed). The two following prepositional phrases give what was acted on and where: بر نامه‌ها 'upon the letters' and بر نگین 'on/with a signet' (here نگین functions as a signet/seal attached to the letters). The verb comes early in this classical/poetic word order and the subject is implied by the verb ending.
segments.s599-g106: فرستادگان is the plural noun 'envoys' and functions here as the object of the verb خواست. خواست is the simple past, 3rd‑person singular of خواستن 'to want/ask' (he wanted/asked), so the subject is implied (contextually the ruler). با is the preposition 'with' and آفرین here means 'acclamation/praise' describing the manner of the envoys or the compliments sent with them.
segments.s599-g107: برفتند is the past tense, 3rd‑person plural of رفتن (they went/they departed). با نامه‌ها is a prepositional phrase 'with the letters' showing accompaniment. موبدان (mōbedān) is a plural noun 'the mōbeds' (Zoroastrian priests) and here is the subject of برفتند.
segments.s599-g108: سواران is a nominative/absolute plural noun 'horsemen' that apposes or further describes the mōbeds; Persian often stacks appositive groups. بینا‌دل is a compound adjective 'bina‑del' (broad‑hearted/open‑hearted) and بخردان is another adjective/noun meaning 'wise, prudent'; و is the coordinating 'and' linking the two qualities. Adjectives follow the noun they modify in this classical ordering.
segments.s599-g9: ازان پس چنین گفت is a small clause meaning 'after that he said thus' (ازان پس = 'after that'; چنین = 'thus, so'; گفت = past verb 'said'). کاین is an archaic fused form (ک + این) introducing the following nominal phrase as the thing spoken: 'this/that crown and throne'. Word order is typical poetic inversion: temporal phrase + reported-speech verb + the introduced object.
segments.s599-g10: ازو یافتم is literal 'from him I found/obtained' with یافتم past 1st‑person 'I obtained' and ازو = از + او ('from him'). که‌آفریده‌ست بخت is a subordinate explanation clause introduced by که ('that/for'), with آفریده‌ست an archaic perfect ('has created' — ست = old form of است) and بخت as the agent 'Fortune'. The verb-before-subject order is a poetic inversion.
segments.s599-g11: بدو هستم امید literally 'to him I am hope' — an idiomatic, somewhat inverted way to say 'I have hope in him' (هستم = 'I am', امید = 'hope', بدو = 'to him'). و هم ... هراس links a contrasting feeling: 'and also (هم) from him (زو) fear' = 'I also dread/fear him.' The pronoun/particle زو is the poetic contraction of از + او.
segments.s599-g12: وزو دارم از نیکوی‌ها سپاس parses as 'and (و) + زو (poetic/contracted = از او / 'of/from him' or 'to him') + دارم (I have) + از نیکوی‌ها (for/from the good deeds/benefactions) + سپاس (thanks).' The verb دارم 'I have' takes سپاس as its object, so the phrase means 'and I give him thanks for his good deeds.' The order places the beneficiary/agent phrase before the object, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s599-g13: شما هم بدو نیز نازش کنید is an imperative/admonition: شما 'you (pl./formal)' + هم/نیز both reinforcing 'also' + بدو 'to him' (indirect object) + ناز + ش (the possessive clitic 'his') → نازش 'his pampering/affection' + کنید (2nd‑person plural polite imperative/subjunctive 'do'). Together it means 'You too treat him with affection.'
segments.s599-g14: بکوشید is an imperative/subjunctive 'strive/try' (2nd‑person plural polite). تا here means 'so that / in order that' and introduces the purpose/result clause. عهد او 'his pledge/oath' places the noun before the possessive pronoun او. نشکنید is the negative 2pl present/subjunctive form 'do not break' (negation prefixed to the verb), so the clause means 'strive so that you do not break his covenant.'
segments.s599-g15: زبان برگشادند ایرانیان is an intransitive past clause: زبان 'tongue/speech' (here 'speech' or 'they opened their mouths to speak') + برگشادند past‑plural 'they opened' + ایرانیان 'the Iranians' (subject placed at the end, a typical poetic inversion). The phrase means 'the Iranians spoke up' or literally 'they opened their tongues.'
segments.s599-g16: که بستیم ما بندگی را میان is the content clause introduced by که ('that'), reporting what they said. بستیم is past 1st‑person plural 'we bound' (بستن = 'to bind'), ما is the explicit subject 'we', بندگی را میان = 'servitude/servitude (را = object marker) between (میان) [us]'. Word order places the verb before the subject and the object phrase after it, typical of poetic style.
segments.s599-g97: نخواهیم is the negative of خواهیم (we want/will) and here means 'we do not wish' or 'we will not'; آگندن is a poetic verb meaning 'to soil/taint' or 'to put' (here sense of placing/putting into is intended); زر = 'gold/wealth'; به + گنج places the action into/into the treasure (به is used with sense 'towards/into'). Word order is subject‑verb/object compressed in poetic style: 'we will not (نخواهیم) [to] put/soil gold into the treasury'.
segments.s599-g98: که introduces a causal clause here ('for/because'); از = 'from'; گنج = 'treasure'; درویش means 'poor person' (not mystical sense here), so گنجِ درویش = 'the poor man's treasure'; ماند is the present/past notion 'remains/is left'; به رنج = 'with/through hardship' (به used adverbially meaning 'with' or 'by'), so the clause says that from the poor man's treasure what remains is hardship.
segments.s599-g99: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایزد = 'God' or 'Providence'; the pronoun 'م' plus را together form مرا ('me') — here split in the edition, marking the object 'me' (I receive the action); زندگانی = 'life'; دهد is the present/subjunctive or future‑oriented verb 'gives' (God grants). The clause means 'If God gives me life'.
segments.s599-g100: برین is the poetic contraction of بر این ('upon/under these'); بر = 'on/upon' (here 'under/under the influence of' the stars); ین is the orthographic/demonstrative element (variant of این); اختران = 'stars' (the senses list in the prompt lacked the correct entry, so marked separately); کامرانی = 'success, prosperity'; دهد repeats 'gives'—so 'he will give success under these stars.'
segments.s599-g101: یکی here means 'a certain one' or 'someone'; رامشی is a noun meaning 'calmness/tranquillity' (an attributive for the following نامه); نامه = 'letter' or 'book/record'; خوانید is a past/poetic reading form 'read/recited' (classical epic usage often presents older verb endings — here 'someone read'); نیز = 'also/too'. Together: 'Someone also read a letter/book of tranquillity.'
segments.s599-g102: کزان = 'from which' (کِه + ز + ان construction) introduces the result/benefit; جاودان = 'eternal'; ارج = 'honour/value'; یابید is a subjunctive/optative form ('may you find/attain' or 'you might attain') used to express the hoped‑for result; و = 'and'; چیز = 'thing/other matter(s)'. The clause expresses a wish/result: 'from which you may attain eternal honour and other things.'
segments.s599-g103: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'; ما = 'us/we' (the sender of the greeting); بر here = 'to/toward' so بر همه پادشاهی درود = 'greetings to all kingdoms/kings'; همه = 'all'; پادشاهی in this salutatory context stands for 'kings/kingship/realm'; درود = 'greeting/blessing.'
segments.s599-g104: به ویژه is the fixed expression 'especially' (به = 'to/for' + ویژه = 'special'); که introduces a causal clause 'because/since'; مهرش = مهر + ش (his love/affection); بود = past of 'to be' (was); تار و پود are weaving terms 'warp and weft' used metaphorically to mean the very fabric or inner structure (i.e., his love formed the warp and weft).
segments.s599-g57: به کوشش is an instrumental/adverbial phrase (به + noun) meaning 'by/with effort'. بجوییم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative (let us seek / we should seek) placed after the adverbial; the normal order here is adverbial + verb. خرم qualifies بهشت (adjective before noun) meaning 'pleasant/joyful Paradise.'
segments.s599-g58: این جملهٔ وصفی begins with خنک ('cool', adjective) qualifying the paradise just mentioned. آنک is the relative pronoun (آن‌که = 'that/which') introducing a relative clause. جز is the preposition 'except', followed by تخم نیکی 'seed of goodness' as the thing excepted. نکشت here functions as the verb in the relative clause; its exact archaic/poetic form is irregular in the provided senses, so the clause reads 'which has not (sown/produced) anything except the seed of goodness.'
segments.s599-g59: گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past), followed by بر + مردم زیردست: بر here indicates 'to/toward' (said to), مردم is the direct addressee, and زیردست ('those under [one's authority]') specifies which people. Word order is verb then prepositional phrase indicating the audience.
segments.s599-g60: مبادا introduces a negative wish/prohibition meaning 'may it not be / let it not happen'. که links the clause. هرگز 'never' is an adverb modifying the verb بجویم (1st-person-plural subjunctive here, 'may we seek' or with مبادا 'let us not seek'); شکست is the object 'defeat/failure'. The clause therefore expresses a hortative negative: 'may we never seek (suffer) defeat.'
segments.s599-g61: جهان را uses را to mark the definite object 'the world'. ز دشمن is a prepositional phrase 'from/from the enemy' (source or cause). تن‌آسان is an adjective meaning 'at ease/comfortable' and کنیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us make' or 'we should make', so the structure literally is 'let us make the world, from the enemy, at ease' = 'let us free the world from the enemy and make it secure.'
segments.s599-g62: بداندیشگان را marks the people with ill intent as the definite object. هراسان is an adjectival predicate 'frightened', and کنیم again is the hortative 'let us make/cause'. The construction is cause + adjective (کن + هراسان) meaning 'let us make the ill-wishers afraid.'
segments.s599-g63: به هفتم here indicates 'to/at the seventh (turn)' — به + ordinal — and چو بنشست 'when he sat' (چو = 'when', بنشست = 'sat down' past). گفت is the reporting verb 'he said'. ای مهان is a vocative 'O guests' addressing the assembly. Word order follows classical Persian narrative: temporal phrase + verb + reporting verb + vocative.
segments.s599-g64: This is a series of epithets in apposition to the addressees: خردمند 'wise', و بیدار 'and awake/alert', و دیده جهان 'and the eye of the world' (دیده here = 'eye' used metaphorically). Adjectives/nouns are simply conjoined with و; they function as direct attributes of the addressed guests.
segments.s599-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” The clause بر تخت بنشست is a past-tense verbal phrase: بر (on) + تخت (throne) + بنشست (sat down), so word order is temporal word → locative phrase → past verb. بهرام گور is the proper name (Bahram with epithet Gūr) following the verb as the subject in classical Persian narrative order.
segments.s599-g2: آفرین کرد is a light verb construction: آفرین (an exclamation/acclamation) + کرد (did/said) = “uttered ‘afarin’/cried ‘bravo’.” The sequence places the exclamation before the verb. The following بهرام و هور names the agents who made the exclamation (Bahrām and Hūr). The initial برو is an interjection/particle here preceding آفرین (in this context functioning like an intensifier or part of the exclamatory phrase) rather than the literal imperative “go.”
segments.s599-g3: پرستش گرفت is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning “took worship” → “worshipped”; گرفت is past tense “took/did.” آفریننده را is the definite object (آفریننده = ‘the Creator’) marked by را. The verb takes the noun phrase پرستش as its object and آفریننده را as the indirect/primary object of worship.
segments.s599-g4: This segment lists three epithets in apposition to آفریننده: جهاندار (world-holder/sovereign), بیدار (the Awake), و بیننده (and the Beholder). The final را (object marker) stands for the whole coordinated phrase, marking the Creator as the definite object; the adjectives/titles are linked by و (and).
segments.s599-g5: خداوند here is used as an epithet/vocative ‘Lord’ and is immediately followed by nouns (پیروزی و برتری) that specify attributes: ‘victory and superiority.’ The order is noun phrase in apposition to the repeated title خداوند.
segments.s599-g6: A repeated خداوند introduces a second pair of attributes: افزونی (increase/abundance) and کمتری (diminution/less). The conjunction و links the two nouns; the repetition of خداوند is a poetic device to enumerate divine domains.
segments.s599-g7: خداوند داد و خداوند رای repeats the title خداوند with two different nouns: داد here is used as the abstract noun ‘justice’ (not the verb ‘gave’), and رای is ‘counsel/opinion’ — so the phrase reads as two epithets: ‘Lord of justice and Lord of counsel.’ The conjunction و links the two nominal phrases.
segments.s599-g8: کزویست is a fused relative/causal phrase meaning ‘for/which is from him’ or ‘that is from him,’ introducing the explanatory clause. گیتی سراسر ‘the whole world’ (گیتی = world, سراسر = entire/throughout) is the subject, and به پای literally ‘to the foot’ means ‘at his feet’ (possessive is implied by the previous reference to him). The clause thus states that the entire world is at his feet.
segments.s599-g33: چنین گفت is a fixed order meaning 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 3rd-person past 'said'); the subject بهرام follows the verb (a common poetic V‑S order). با مهتران is a prepositional phrase 'with the elders/chiefs' (با = 'with', مهتران = plural, here 'chiefs/elders').
segments.s599-g34: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'who' leading into what he said). این points to the following people ('these'); نیکنامان and نیک‌اختران are coordinated nouns: 'the reputable' + 'the well-starred/fortunate (people)'.
segments.s599-g35: به یزدان گراییم is a first-person-plural hortative/optative 'let us turn to God' (گراییم from گرا, present/subjunctive sense). رامش کنیم combines the noun رامش 'solace/tranquility' with the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/imperative کنیم 'let us make/do' to mean 'let us find/make peace/solace.'
segments.s599-g36: بتازیم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive of تاختن 'to rush/ride/gallop' — here hortative 'let us rush forth.' و links the clauses. دل functions as the direct object 'the heart' (literal heart/soul). زین here is the preposition-like poetic contraction (ز این = 'from this'); given the provided sense list does not match that use, it is treated as the marker 'from this'; جهان = 'this world/this life'. برکنیم (1st-plural subjunctive of برکندن) = 'let us remove/throw off' — 'let us cast our heart off this world.'
segments.s599-g37: بگفت is the simple past 3rd-person 'he said' (note the poetic form). این is the object 'this' (referring to what was just spoken). The conjunction و links to the next clause. اسپ کیان خواستند is a clause 'they asked for the Kian steed(s)' with خواستند as 3rd-person-plural past 'they wanted/asked (for)'.
segments.s599-g38: کیی here denotes the Kayanian/royal (adjectival/national epithet). بارگاهش is 'his/its court/palace' (بارگاه + possessive -ش). بیاراستند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they adorned/arrayed' — a completed action describing preparation of the court.
segments.s599-g39: سه دیگر ('three others' / 'the other three') is the noun phrase indicating who acted. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنشست is past 3rd-person singular 'sat down' (here 'sat on the throne'). بر تخت is the locative phrase 'upon the throne'. گفت is past 'he said' introducing the following reported clause.
segments.s599-g40: که introduces reported speech 'that'. رسم پرستش combines رسم 'custom/rite' with پرستش 'worship' to mean 'the rite/custom of worship'. نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not' and نهفت is an adjective/verb 'hidden/concealed' — together 'that the rite of worship must not be hidden.'
segments.s599-g25: ز بدروز بی‌بیم داریمتان is a compact clause in which ز (a poetic preposition 'from') introduces the source/condition (بدروز, 'ill-day/misfortune'). بی‌بیم ('without fear' = 'fearless') is an adjectival/adverbial phrase modifying داریمتان. داریمتان is a present/persistent stative verb with enclitic ـتان ('you') meaning 'we keep/hold you' — so literally 'we keep you free from misfortune, fearless.'
segments.s599-g26: به بدخواه حاجت نیاریمتان: به introduces the indirect relation ('to/for'); بدخواه ('ill-wisher') and حاجت ('need') form the object-complement 'need of an ill-wisher.' نیاریمتان is a negative-present/future-leaning verb with the 1st-person-plural subject implied and enclitic ـتان, here meaning 'we will not bring/subject you (to such need),' so the phrase idiomatically means 'we will not put you in need at the hands of ill-wishers.'
segments.s599-g27: بگفت این و از پیش برخاستند is two coordinated parts: بگفت ('he said') + این ('this') — 'he said this' — followed by و ('and/then') linking to از پیش برخاستند. از پیش + برخاستند literally 'rose from before (him),' where از پیش means 'from the presence of' and برخاستند is past plural 'they rose/stood up/left.'
segments.s599-g28: برو آفرین نو آراستند is an expression describing an acclamation: برو here functions as the exclamatory element attached to the shout, آفرین ('bravo/praise') is the cry, نو ('new') qualifies that cry, and آراستند (they arranged/made) is a past plural verb meaning 'they produced/uttered.' So together: 'they made/uttered a new acclamation of “bravo.”'
segments.s599-g29: شب تیره بودند با گفت‌وگوی is a verbless/nominal predication turned finite by بودند ('they were'): شب تیره ('a dark night') is the subject phrase, بودند places it in past tense ('it/they were a dark night'), and با گفت‌وگوی ('with conversation') is an instrumental/circumstantial phrase explaining how the night was spent — 'they were (in) a dark night with conversation' i.e. 'they spent a dark night conversing.'
segments.s599-g30: چو خورشید بر چرخ بنمود روی is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو ('when/as'). خورشید is the subject, بر چرخ ('upon the sky/heaven') is the locative phrase, بنمود is a past-tense verb 'showed/revealed,' and روی ('face') is the direct object — 'when the sun showed its face upon the sky.'
segments.s599-g31: به آرام بنشست بر گاه شاه: به آرام ('quietly/calmly') is an adverbial phrase modifying بنشست ('he sat down' — past singular). بر گاه شاه is a locative phrase where بر + گاه ('on the seat/at the throne') is specified by شاه ('the king'), so the whole means 'he sat down calmly on the king's seat/at the royal court.'
segments.s599-g32: برفتند ایرانیان بارخواه is an example of poetic word order (verb before subject): برفتند ('they went/advanced') is past plural; ایرانیان ('the Iranians') is the subject; بارخواه ('petitioners/seeking a grant') qualifies them. Together: 'the Iranian petitioners went forward/approached.'
segments.s599-g41: به is the preposition 'to/for' introducing the recipient frame; هستی یزدان is a genitive construction 'the existence/being of God' (هستی + یزدان). گوایی is a noun meaning 'speech/eloquence' or 'a voice' and functions as the thing being given; دهیم is first‑person plural present/jussive 'we give' and sits at the end as the verb of the clause (typical Persian SOV ordering).
segments.s599-g42: روان را is the marked definite object ('the soul', with را after روان). بدین (here written as ب + دین in the input) is an adverbial/compound meaning 'by/with this' or 'to this', and آشنایی is the noun 'acquaintance/familiarity' which is the thing given; دهیم repeats the verb 'we give', linking this second object to the same subject. Word order: object + adverbial + verb, with را marking the object.
segments.s599-g43: بهشت‌ست is a nominal with the copula -ست ('is Heaven'). و هم introduces additional items ('and also' / 'likewise'); دوزخ and رستخیز are coordinated nouns 'Hell' and 'Resurrection'. The copula is attached to the first noun and the rest of the list follows, a common poetic compression.
segments.s599-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (used twice: ز نیک و ز بد = 'from good and from bad'). نیست is the negative copula 'there is not / there is no', and راه گریز is a noun phrase 'the way (راه) of escape (گریز)' where گریز postmodifies راه. The overall order is prepositional phrases + negative copula + nominal predicate.
segments.s599-g45: کسی is the indefinite 'anyone / someone'; کاو is an archaic relative 'who' (classical Persian ka- / ka-ū). نگرود is a classical third‑person form meaning 'turns to / adopts' (here negated in sense by the context — 'who does not turn'); به‌روز شمار is a compound meaning roughly 'counted among the day' (i.e. reckoned for the day, 'counted as living or belonging to the day'). The clause is a relative clause modifying 'کسی'.
segments.s599-g46: مر is the archaic object pronoun 'to me' (marking the speaker's standpoint); او را is the direct object 'him' with the object marker را. تو is the addressee 'you' introducing the imperative direction; با‌دین و دانا are adjectival predicates 'pious and wise'. مدار here functions as an (archaic/formal) prohibitive/imperative meaning 'do not regard/consider' — so the whole clause tells the addressee not to consider that person pious or wise in the speaker's estimation.
segments.s599-g47: به‌روز چهارم is a temporal phrase 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز = 'on the day/of the day' + ordinal). چو is the conjunction 'when', and بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'upon the ivory throne' (بر + تخت, modified by عاج 'ivory'). This segment sets the time and scene for the action that follows.
segments.s599-g48: بسر بر نهاد is an archaic word order of the common phrase بر سر نهاد 'placed (upon) the head': نهاد is the past tense 'placed/put', بر is the preposition 'on', and بسر here participates in the idiom 'بر سر نهاد' (put on the head). آن پسندیده تاج is the noun phrase 'that chosen/approved crown' (آن 'that' + پسندیده 'chosen/pleasing' + تاج 'crown'). The verb نهاد governs the following noun phrase as its object and the clause is third‑person singular past.
segments.s599-g17: This clause is an optative wish introduced by که followed by a clause that ends in the optative particle باد. Word order is roughly ‘this crown upon (the) king auspicious be‑may’: این تاج (subject) + بر شاه (prepositional phrase ‘upon the king’) + فرخنده (epithet after the noun, a poetic order) and the verb/optative باد at the end marks the wish.
segments.s599-g18: همیشه is an adverb fronting the clause (‘always’). دل و بخت او is a coordinated noun phrase (‘his heart and his fortune’) where او functions as a separate possessive pronoun (poetic emphasis instead of the enclitic). زنده‌باد is the optative formula ‘long live / may ... live’ that gives the whole clause its wishful meaning.
segments.s599-g19: وزان پس is a temporal phrase meaning ‘from that time/thereafter’ (وزان = ‘from that’, پس = ‘after/then’). همه آفرین خواندند is a past-tense, third-person-plural action: آفرین here is the exclamation/acclamation and خواندند is the past plural verb ‘they uttered/sang/cried’ (used idiomatically as ‘they cried “bravo”’).
segments.s599-g20: همه (all/everyone) is the subject; بر سرش is the prepositional phrase ‘upon his head’ where ش is the possessive clitic ‘his’; گوهر is the direct object ‘gems/jewels’; افشاندند is the past plural verb ‘they scattered/sprinkled’ — a coronation‑type image ('they showered jewels upon his head').
segments.s599-g21: چنین گفت is a formula ‘thus said’ (چنین = thus, گفت = said), with بهرام as the speaker. کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ addressing the following noun; سرکشان is a vocative plural meaning ‘the proud/rebellious ones’ — the whole phrase performs direct address: ‘Thus Bahram said, “O proud ones…”’.
segments.s599-g22: ز governs the following noun phrase ‘نیک و بد روز’ = ‘good and bad days’ (ز = ‘from/of’ in poetic usage). دیده is here a past participle/participial adjective ‘having seen’ (literally ‘seen’), and نشان is ‘mark/sign’ — together the phrase characterizes those addressed as ones who bear the marks of having experienced good and bad days (‘you who have seen both good and bad days’).
segments.s599-g23: همه بندگانیم is a first‑person‑plural nominal sentence: همه (all/everyone) + بندگان (servants) + ‑یم (we are) — ‘we are all servants’. و links the clauses; ایزد یکی‌ست is a predicate with the archaic copula ست (‘is’): ‘God is one.’
segments.s599-g24: پرستش is the noun ‘worship’; جز او را is the exception phrase ‘except him’ with را marking the definite/focused object (را attaches after جز + pronoun in this poetic order); سزاوار نیست is a predicate ‘is not deserving/apt’ (سزاوار = deserving, نیست = is not). Together: ‘Worship except Him is not deserving’ → ‘No one is worthy of worship except Him.’
segments.s599-g65: چو is the subordinating particle 'when/if' introducing a conditional/temporal clause. با مردم is a prepositional phrase 'with/regarding the people' (با + noun). زفت زفتی is a reduplicated adjective/nominal phrase meaning 'nasty/filthy behavior' (زفت 'nasty' + adjectival/nominal -ی), and کنیم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/optative of کردن 'to do' — here 'we do/let us do' used to state the action under the condition.
segments.s599-g66: همی is a poetic particle (here an emphatic/continuous marker, 'indeed/then' or ongoing) placed before the verb. با خردمند جفتی کنیم is a simple clause: با + noun ('with the wise') + جفتی 'as a pair/paired' (adjectival/adverbial) + کنیم (we do) — literally 'we make (ourselves) paired with the wise.'
segments.s599-g67: هرانکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever that...' (هرانکس = 'whoever' + que/که). با ما نسازند uses با + ما 'with us' and نسازند (3rd-plural) here means 'do not get along/agree' (the lexeme ساختن used idiomatically here); the grammatical subject is generic 'whoever'. گرم is an adverb/particle following the clause; its exact emphatic/nuanced meaning is idiomatic/poetic and modifies the following result clause.
segments.s599-g68: بدی بیش ازان بیند او is a prediction: بدی 'evil/harm' + بیش 'more' + ازان 'than that' + بیند (3rd-sg present/future 'will see/suffer') + او 'he'. کز پدرم is a relative/agent phrase 'that/from my father' (کز = 'which/from which'; پدرم = 'my father'). The whole clause says the person will suffer worse harm than that which is associated with the speaker's father.
segments.s599-g69: هرانکس که ... برگزید = 'whoever that chooses/accepts': هرانکس 'whoever', که introduces the relative clause, فرمان ما 'our command' (noun + possessive), and برگزید is a past-tense verb 'chose/has chosen' used generically to mean 'accepts/opts for' in this kind of proverb-like statement.
segments.s599-g70: غم و درد و رنجش is a triad of nouns linked by و 'and' (sorrow, pain, distress). نباید is the impersonal modal 'one should not/it must not', and کشید is the verbal infinitive/finite-form meaning 'to bear/endure' used with نباید to mean 'should not have to endure'.
segments.s599-g71: به هشتم is a temporal/locative phrase 'at the eighth' (literal 'to/at the eighth'); چو بنشست is the temporal clause 'when he sat down' (چو = 'when', بنشست = past 'sat down'). فرمود شاه inverts subject and verb in typical poetic order: فرمود 'he commanded/said' + شاه 'the king' (subject).
segments.s599-g72: جوانوی را خواندن از بارگاه is an object-infinitive construction: جوانوی را (object-marked 'the young man') + خواندن here means 'to call/summon' (one of خواندن's senses) + از بارگاه 'from the court/palace' (از = 'from', بارگاه = 'royal court/palace'). The sense is 'have the youth called/summoned from the court.'
segments.s599-g73: بدو گفت = بدو (to him) + گفت (said); this is a short reporting clause meaning “he said to him.” نزدیک هر مهتری follows as a directive: نزدیک here has the sense 'approach/come near' (an intransitive/imperative-like notion) and هر مهتری means “every chief/elder.” Word order is typical Classical Persian: indirect object (بدو) before the reporting verb (گفت), then the following phrase expresses the action to be taken.
segments.s599-g74: به هر نامداری و هر کشوری is a continuation of the destination/recipients: به (to) introduces the targets, هر نامداری “every notable/famous person” and هر کشوری “every country/realm.” The sequence is a simple prepositional phrase listing where/whom he should go to or whose attention to seek.
segments.s599-g75: یکی نامه بنویس = یکی (one/a single) + نامه (letter) + بنویس (imperative 'write'). This is an imperative instruction to compose one letter. با مهر و داد is an instrumental phrase 'with/using مهر and داد'; here مهر is best read as 'favour/kindness' or the proper royal seal/insignia used on letters, and داد means 'justice' or 'righteousness' — together they describe the authoritative form the letter should bear.
segments.s599-g76: که introduces the content clause of the letter: 'that Bahram ...' بهرام is the subject and بنشست is a past-tense perfective verb meaning 'sat down' or 'took his seat.' بر تخت شاد is a prepositional phrase 'upon the joyous throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne, شاد = joyous). The whole clause reports what the letter should state.
segments.s599-g77: This is a nominal equational phrase: خداوند (the Lord/God) followed by بخشایش و راستی as predicate nouns describing divine attributes ('forgiveness and uprightness/truth'). No finite copula is required in Persian poetry; the nouns form an apposition/predicate to خداوند.
segments.s599-g78: گریزنده is a present-participial adjective 'fleeing/shunning' that modifies (or predicates of) God; از introduces the objects avoided, کژی و کاستی ('crookedness and deficiency'). Together they mean God shuns crookedness and lack — a descriptive relative/participial construction.
segments.s599-g79: که begins a relative clause ('who'). با فر و برزست is a possessive/qualifying phrase: با introduces the attributes 'فر' (farr, royal glory) and 'برزست' (exalted/high); the second با مَهر و داد repeats the instrumental 'with' to add 'favour (or seal) and justice.' The repeated با emphasizes the means/qualities by which the subject is characterized.
segments.s599-g80: نگیرد جز از = نـ (negative) + گیرد (takes/accepts) → 'does not take/accept except from.' This is a restrictive negation pattern meaning 'accepts nothing except ...' پاک دادار یاد is the source/exclusive exception: پاک (pure) + دادار (the Giver/Creator) + یاد (remembrance). Grammatically this clause states that the subject takes/accepts only the remembrance of the Pure Creator.
segments.s599-g89: بران is a poetic compound meaning 'concerning/with respect to that' (literally 'بر آن'); دین زردشت is a noun phrase 'the religion of Zoroaster'. پیغمبرم is پیغمبر + the enclitic -م 'my', so 'my prophet' (appositive identifying Zoroaster). The whole segment names the object/topic the speaker will not abandon.
segments.s599-g90: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. راه نیاکان خود is a genitive phrase 'the way of (my) ancestors' where خود is reflexive/ emphatic 'own'. نگذرم is the first‑person present negative form of گذشتن used here as an emphatic 'I will not / I do not depart' (poetic use of ن‑ + present forms to express refusal). The clause gives the action that does not happen to the topic in the previous segment.
segments.s599-g91: گفت is the past/perfect verb 'said' (here used narratively); the preceding نهم (not listed among the words) functions as 'then/next' or 'again'. زردشت is the speaker's name. پیشین can be an adverb 'formerly/earlier' or here indicates 'first/previously' in sequence; بروی is the second‑person singular subjunctive/imperative form of رفتن 'to go' ('go' or 'may you go'). Together they report Zoroaster's utterance directing someone to go.
segments.s599-g92: براهیم is the proper name 'Abraham', followed by appositional پیغمبر 'prophet'. راست‌گوی is a compound adjective 'truth‑speaking' = 'truthful/honest' modifying 'prophet'. This is an appositive phrase identifying the person addressed or referenced in the imperative of the previous segment.
segments.s599-g93: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; پادشاهید is the plural/polite present 'you are kings' or generically 'are rulers'. بر introduces the domain 'over/on' and چیز خوی‌ش (split orthographically into خوی + ش) means 'their own thing(s)' — خوی 'kin/one's own' plus enclitic ش 'his/her/their'. The phrase says each rules over his own affairs.
segments.s599-g94: نگهبان 'guardian/protector' is used predicatively and repeated in coordination. مرز 'border' (frontier) and کیش 'creed/sect' are the two objects of guardianship, joined by و 'and'. Word order places the role (guardian) before the things guarded.
segments.s599-g95: به introduces the beneficiaries 'to/for'. فرزند و زن 'child and woman' (زن here = 'wife') are coordinated recipients; نیز هم 'also/too' reinforces inclusion. پادشا (poetic form of پادشاه) 'king' is the agent/subject that the clause attributes behavior to. The cluster says the king should extend his behavior to family as well.
segments.s599-g96: خنک is an adjective here meaning 'kind/pleasant/calm' describing the king's manner toward the following group. مردم 'people' is qualified by زیرک و پارسا 'clever and pious', a coordinated adjectival description. The copula is omitted (typical in poetic Persian), so the structure reads as 'king (be) kind to clever and pious people.'

sentences.s599-s25: He said thus: from my treasure, for a time, I am not happy; I am not rejoicing because the people are merry.
sentences.s599-s26: Partly desirous of a house of repose, not from returning to care and toil.
sentences.s599-s27: For that is eternal and this (one) transient; you, refrain from greed and do not be sorrowful.
sentences.s599-s28: To the fifth he said thus: I am not glad at another’s suffering; I will not be half-happy until I am within reach (to help).
sentences.s599-s41: I accepted that he obeyed the command; what sin is there in that dog‑attacker who took a remedy?
sentences.s599-s42: I sat upon this throne of the fortunate father, in the manner of Tahmuras the just.
sentences.s599-s43: I will increase justice beyond my ancestors; I will guide you in the faith.
sentences.s599-s44: I deal with everyone only in uprightness; if, however, some fault comes from him, that is enough.
sentences.s599-s53: They set signets upon the letters, and he asked for envoys with acclamation.
sentences.s599-s54: The mōbeds went off with the letters — horsemen, broad‑hearted and wise.
sentences.s599-s5: After that he said thus: "I received this crown and throne from him, for Fortune has created them."
sentences.s599-s6: I place my hope in him and also fear him; for his good deeds I give thanks to him.
sentences.s599-s7: You too show him affection, and strive so that you do not break his oath.
sentences.s599-s8: The Iranians opened their tongues (spoke up), saying that 'we have bound servitude between us.'
sentences.s599-s49: We will not put gold into the treasury, for from the poor man's treasure only hardship remains.
sentences.s599-s50: If God grants me life, he will grant me success under these stars.
sentences.s599-s51: A certain one also read a book of calm, so that from it you might attain eternal honor and other things.
sentences.s599-s52: From us greetings to all the kings, especially since his affection was the warp and the weft.
sentences.s599-s29: Let us, by exertion, seek the pleasant Paradise — the cool one that has sown nothing but the seed of goodness.
sentences.s599-s30: The sixth said to the people beneath (him), 'May we never seek defeat.'
sentences.s599-s31: Let us rid the world of the enemy and put the world at ease; let us frighten those of evil intent.
sentences.s599-s32: When the seventh sat down he said, 'O guests, wise, awake, and eyes of the world...'
sentences.s599-s1: When Bahram Gūr sat upon the throne, Bahram and Hūr cried “Bravo.”
sentences.s599-s2: He worshipped the Creator — the sovereign, the Awake, the Beholder.
sentences.s599-s3: Lord of victory and supremacy, Lord of abundance and of diminution.
sentences.s599-s4: Lord of justice and Lord of counsel — for from him the whole world lies at (his) feet.
sentences.s599-s17: Thus Bahram said to the elders: 'These reputable and well-starred ones...'
sentences.s599-s18: Let us turn to God and seek solace; let us rush forth and free the heart from this world.
sentences.s599-s19: He said this; they asked for the Kian steed(s), and the Kayanian nobles adorned his court.
sentences.s599-s20: When three others sat upon the throne, he said that the rite of worship must not be concealed.
sentences.s599-s13: We keep you free from ill-days and fearless; we will not expose you to need at the hands of ill-wishers.
sentences.s599-s14: He said this, and they rose from his presence; then they raised a fresh cry of 'bravo.'
sentences.s599-s15: They passed the dark night in conversation; when the sun showed its face upon the sky...
sentences.s599-s16: He sat down calmly on the king's seat; the Iranians who came to petition advanced.
sentences.s599-s21: We give God's being a voice, and we give the soul acquaintance with this.
sentences.s599-s22: There is Heaven, and likewise Hell and the Resurrection; there is no way of escape from good or from evil.
sentences.s599-s23: Do not reckon as counted any man who does not turn to the day; as for him, do not deem him pious and wise in my sight.
sentences.s599-s24: On the fourth day, when he sat upon the ivory throne, he placed that chosen crown upon his head.
sentences.s599-s9: May this crown be upon the auspicious king, and may his heart and fortune live forever.
sentences.s599-s10: From that time on everyone cried out 'bravo', and they showered jewels upon his head.
sentences.s599-s11: Thus Bahram said, 'O proud ones, you who have seen both good and bad days.'
sentences.s599-s12: We are all servants and God is one; worship is due to none but Him.
sentences.s599-s33: If we treat the people nastily, we should pair ourselves with the wise.
sentences.s599-s34: Whoever will not get along with us will see a greater evil than that from my father.
sentences.s599-s35: Whoever accepts our command should not have to endure sorrow, pain, or distress.
sentences.s599-s36: When he sat down at the eighth (place), the king commanded that the young man be called from the court.
sentences.s599-s37: He said to him, “Go up to every chief, to every notable and to every realm.”
sentences.s599-s38: Write a letter — one — sealed with favour and justice, saying that Bahram has taken his seat upon the joyous throne.
sentences.s599-s39: God (is) forgiveness and uprightness, shunning crookedness and defect.
sentences.s599-s40: Who, with glory and exaltation, and with favour and justice, accepts nothing except from the remembrance of the Pure Creator.
sentences.s599-s45: I will not abandon the religion of Zoroaster, my prophet, nor leave the way of my ancestors.
sentences.s599-s46: Then Zoroaster spoke: 'Go before Abraham, the truthful prophet.'
sentences.s599-s47: All of you are kings over your own possessions, guardians of the border and guardians of the faith.
sentences.s599-s48: Let the king also be kind to his children and wife, and to people who are clever and pious.
